summary: Jeff Atwood admits he didn't fully grasp the importance of net neutrality until reading Tim Wu's 'The Master Switch,' which traces how communication networks from telegraph to television have been repeatedly captured by monopolies. He recounts the absurd 1950s legal battle where AT&T tried to ban customers from attaching a simple plastic cup (the Hush-a-Phone) to their telephones, illustrating monopolistic overreach. Fascinatingly, the expert witnesses who fought AT&T in that case—Licklider and Beranek—later designed ARPANET, the internet's predecessor. Atwood argues the internet was uniquely designed to resist centralized control, but historical patterns show open networks tend to become closed as they commercialize. He urges vigilance in protecting net neutrality, warning that even beloved devices can be turned against users.
key_points: Net neutrality ensures all internet content is treated equally, with intelligence and control at the edges rather than in network owners' hands
The history of communication networks (telegraph, radio, telephone, TV) shows a recurring pattern: they start open and swing closed through commercialization and monopolization
AT&T's attempt to ban the Hush-a-Phone—a simple plastic cup attachment—exemplifies how monopolies abuse power to control even trivial innovations
The pioneers who fought AT&T's monopoly (Licklider and Beranek) went on to design ARPANET, a system built to resist centralized control
Consumer devices like iPads and Kindles can become instruments of control if net neutrality protections erode
money_quotes: I never fully understood the importance of net neutrality until last week.
All of the intelligence and control is held by producers and users, not the networks that connect them.

Without historical context, it's impossible to appreciate how scarily easy it is for common carriage to get subverted and undermined by corporations and government in subtle (and sometimes not so subtle) ways, with terrible long-term consequences for society.
These gentlemen went on from battling the Bell monopoly in court in the 1950s to designing a system in 1968 that would ultimately defeat it: the internet.

The internet is radically unlike all the telecommunications networks that have preceded it. It's the first national and global communication network designed from the outset to resist mechanisms for centralized control and monopoly.
Historically speaking, all communication networks start out open and then rapidly swing closed as they are increasingly commercialized.

If we do not take this moment to secure our sovereignty over the choices that our information age has allowed us to enjoy, we cannot reasonably blame its loss on those who are free to enrich themselves by taking it from us in a manner history has foretold.
Even devices that you may love, like an iPad, Kindle, or Xbox, can easily be turned against you – if you let them.
key_insight: The internet's open architecture was a deliberate response to the monopolistic capture of previous communication networks, making net neutrality not just a policy preference but a defense against historically inevitable corporate enclosure.
